episode_meta.title: Gina Chua: Where Journalism's Value Lives When AI Tells the Story

ai_tools: Google Suite / App Scripts (for vibe coding AI tools)
Lynx (Reuters CMS, non-AI but referenced in workflow context)
System 77 (Reuters legacy CMS, referenced in workflow context)
Jess (Journalist Editorial Safety Support tool, RAG-based chatbot)

use_cases.description: Built a copy editing bot to assist with editing stories
Built a style guide bot to check stories against Semafor's style guide
use_cases.tool_used: LLM / Google App Scripts with Claude
use_cases.outcome: Worked 'reasonably well'; currently being rebuilt with vibe coding in Google Suite

use_cases.description: MISO: multilingual search tool that translates search terms into multiple languages, searches for stories in those languages, translates and summarizes them to help reporters access a broad range of sources quickly
use_cases.outcome: Let reporters sift through more information more quickly; summaries not fully trusted but useful for efficiency

use_cases.description: Vibe coding AI tools using Claude and Google App Scripts, building shared architecture with shared search service and shared RAG service
use_cases.outcome: Enabled non-engineers to rapidly build and iterate on internal AI tools

use_cases.description: Tool that reads a story, summarizes it, turns it into a search term, and finds related stories in any language for research purposes
use_cases.outcome: Helps reporters do research across languages

use_cases.description: Planned news literacy tool: input a search term, find stories from five different organizations, summarize agreed-upon facts, highlight unique facts per organization, and show how each organization framed the story
use_cases.outcome: Still hitting glitches because search tool doesn't pull in entire stories

use_cases.description: Planned story deconstructor tool: input any story and it extracts verifiable facts, assumptions/analysis, and framing
use_cases.outcome: Concept stage; intended to test LLM contextual understanding limits

use_cases.description: RAG-based query tool on Semafor content to get context across multiple stories
use_cases.outcome: Very good at bringing different context to different readers

use_cases.description: Hate crime classifier: used DOJ definition of hate crime as RAG input, scored made-up stories on 1-5 scale for likelihood of being a hate crime
use_cases.outcome: Performed well with strong contextual information; seen as applicable to content moderation

use_cases.description: Trans misinformation and misframing bot: input a story and it identifies misinformation and misframing around trans issues based on curated reference documents
use_cases.outcome: Could identify not just misinformation in a story but also what was missing (misframing); technique applicable to immigration, deportations, etc.

use_cases.description: Jess (Journalist Editorial Safety Support): RAG-based chatbot built on Mike Christie's security tip sheets and guidance documents from Reuters, providing journalist safety advice in multiple languages
use_cases.organization: Collaboration between Gina Chua, Mike Christie, Joel Simon (CUNY), Elizabeth (ACOS Alliance), and Nikita Roy
use_cases.outcome: Democratizes access to safety knowledge previously only available at large newsrooms like Reuters; works in Hindi, Tagalog, Nepali, French, Portuguese, and more

use_cases.description: Voice cloning for a friend with ALS: found clips of his old voice, cloned it, built a handheld app so he can speak in his own voice; plan to record a lecture series using the cloned voice
use_cases.outcome: Enabled a friend losing speech function to communicate in his own voice; plan for five-part lecture series

use_cases.description: Rule-based (non-LLM) automated story generation at Reuters and AP that produced large volumes of stories
use_cases.outcome: Generated large volumes of stories with a measurable error rate (cited as hypothetically around 0.5%)

use_cases.description: Using Claude as a writing assistant for longer-form pieces, helping connect ideas and find structure
use_cases.outcome: Found Claude's facility with language helpful for developing ideas and connecting themes

key_claims.claim: LLM models are not stable; you build something, it works fine for three weeks, then stops working because the underlying training has changed
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: The biggest deficit the news industry has is imagination — newsrooms are not thinking big enough about what AI can enable
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: A lot of narration work in journalism is going to be done by machines, and journalism's real value lies in asking questions, uncovering facts, understanding audiences, and building relationships — the front end of the value chain
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: The vast majority of a newspaper is 400-word stories that are functionally as close to written by a machine as you could imagine
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: Completely AI-created artifacts are currently not copyrightable under existing legal understanding, which has implications for journalism since you can copyright the form of words but not facts
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: When Homicide Watch DC was founded, there were about 150 murders a year in DC (now about 260), and the Washington Post only covers a subset, meaning many families' stories go untold
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: Meta is working on capability to build customized advertising on the fly using AI, as reported by The Wall Street Journal
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: Reuters spent roughly 30 years trying to change its CMS through multiple failed experiments costing millions of dollars before finally succeeding with Lynx in 2011
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: If the error rate of AI-generated security advice is compared against the baseline of having no security advice at all, even imperfect advice is an improvement
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: Agentic AI workflows are coming much faster than expected — a year ago Gina would have said five years, now she thinks a year and a half to two years
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: The adoption problem for AI tools in newsrooms is fundamentally a social engineering and workflow integration problem, not a technology problem
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

key_claims.claim: Journalism has historically underserved many communities including coverage of civil rights, AIDS, LGBT issues, and women's suffrage
key_claims.speaker: Gina Chua

challenges.description: LLM models are unstable — tools break after underlying model training changes without warning
AI tools don't integrate smoothly into existing newsroom workflows, reducing adoption and effectiveness
Tension between two approaches to tool adoption: making tools that look like existing systems (easier adoption) vs. forcing new workflows (better outcomes but uphill struggle)
Search tools don't pull in entire stories, limiting the ability to build tools that need full article text for comparison and analysis
AI-generated content may not be copyrightable, threatening journalism's business model which relies on copyrighting the form of words
Skepticism and resistance from journalists who are set in their ways, requiring a phased adoption strategy from enthusiasts to the reluctant middle to forced adoption for holdouts
Concern about life-and-death reliability of AI safety advice tools (Jess) — raised by audience member from the New York Times
Loss of editorial voice and byline attribution when AI remixes content from multiple sources into personalized formats
Business model disruption: if AI intermediates information delivery, how do news organizations pay the bills?
Journalism industry layoffs attributed to AI (Business Insider cited as laying off 21% of staff), creating fear and resistance
Voice cloning for ALS friend was difficult because the person's current voice is no longer stable enough for speech recognition
Difficulty trusting LLM summaries — reporters could use MISO summaries to sift through information but did not necessarily trust the summaries themselves

quotes.text: We're not in the business of saving journalists. We're not necessarily even in the business of saving journalism. We're in the business of saving information in the public interest.
quotes.speaker: Gina Chua
quotes.topic: The core mission of journalism in the AI era

quotes.text: We go to war with the audience we have, not the audience we would like to have.
quotes.speaker: Gina Chua
quotes.topic: Adapting to changing audience behavior driven by AI

quotes.text: The biggest deficit we have as an industry is imagination. We're not thinking big enough.
quotes.speaker: Gina Chua

quotes.text: We are now in a place, in a position, in a time, you call it an opportunity or you can call it a rolling disaster, whichever you want to call it. But the world is going to change... If we're going to reinvent journalism, let's burn it to the studs and think about what we should do and can do and who we can truly serve.
quotes.speaker: Gina Chua

quotes.text: An LLM is a smart, slightly autistic, but very friendly English major. I call him Dylan. He has a beret and a cigarette holder. He slept through every class, but he's very good at English and a bunch of other languages.
quotes.speaker: Gina Chua
quotes.topic: Explaining LLM capabilities and limitations through analogy

quotes.text: The only thing I'd say is just do not bet on it being the same, because that is a losing bet.
quotes.speaker: Gina Chua
quotes.topic: Inevitability of change in the news industry

recommendations.advice: Start by figuring out what is good for people (the audience) and then figure out how journalism can play a role in bringing it to them — don't start from the business model
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Think of AI tools in three categories: things that help humans do what they already do, things that help humans do things at scale no amount of humans could do, and things AI can do that humans cannot
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Use RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) as the core pattern for building trustworthy newsroom AI tools — give the LLM curated reference documents rather than relying on its training data
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Adopt a phased strategy for newsroom AI adoption: bring on the enthusiasts first, make it easy enough for the middle majority to play, then change the tools so holdouts have no choice
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Keep testing the edges of what LLMs do well and what they do badly — treat it like testing a new employee's skill set
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Do not bet on the news industry staying the same — that is a losing bet
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Compare AI tool error rates against the realistic baseline (e.g., no security advisor at all), not against a gold standard that most people don't have access to
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Journalism education should focus on core skills of how information systems work and how to extract information, combined with a strong sense of mission, then let young journalists loose to innovate
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Invest in design thinking, workflow design, and social engineering when rolling out AI tools — the adoption problem is more social than technical
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

recommendations.advice: Build tight, authentic relationships with audiences through voice-driven journalism — that relationship is harder to disrupt than commodity news
recommendations.speaker: Gina Chua

external_references: {'title': 'Homicide Watch DC', 'type': 'project/publication', 'description': 'A husband-and-wife journalism project (Chris had a day job, so effectively 1.5 people) that covered every murder in the District of Columbia, unlike larger outlets like the Washington Post which only cover a subset'}

{'title': "The Wall Street Journal report on Meta's AI-generated customized advertising", 'type': 'news article', 'description': 'WSJ story about Meta working on the capability to build customized advertising on the fly using AI, referenced by Gina Chua as an indicator of where personalized content is headed'}

{'title': 'Careless People (book)', 'type': 'book', 'description': 'Referenced by Gina Chua — the first chapter describes someone desperately trying to read everything about the New Zealand earthquake, used to illustrate the need for AI-powered news aggregation and deduplication'}

{'title': 'Perugia Journalism Festival', 'type': 'event', 'description': 'Where Gina Chua originally made her statement about not being in the business of saving journalists, approximately two to three years prior to this recording'}
{'title': 'Humans in the Loop', 'type': 'organization', 'description': 'AI networking group that co-organized the live event at NYU where this episode was recorded'}
{'title': 'ACOS Alliance', 'type': 'organization', 'description': 'Organization involved in the Jess safety bot project; Elizabeth Kentus from ACOS Alliance is contributing to the project'}
{'title': 'CUNY (City University of New York)', 'type': 'organization', 'description': 'Joel Simon at CUNY is managing the Jess journalist safety bot project'}

{'title': 'Business Insider layoffs', 'type': 'news event', 'description': 'Referenced as having laid off 21% of staff, attributed in part to AI, cited by Nikita Roy as an example of industry disruption'}

{'title': "Google's AI hairdresser appointment demo (Google Duplex)", 'type': 'technology demo', 'description': 'Referenced by Gina Chua as an early example of conversational AI that she wrote a blog post about, envisioning it as the future of news consumption'}

{'title': 'Semafor Signals', 'type': 'product/format', 'description': "Semafor's news format that aggregates interesting analysis from other news organizations on a given topic, powered by the MISO tool and partly funded by Microsoft"}
